Now, Delgado is among some 70 workers who've set up a protest camp outside Lexmark, located just 10 miles south of the Texas border.
They also claim they were fired soon after demanding higher pay.
They say they're tired of struggling to provide for their families on a salary that, according to government figures, puts them below the poverty line.
(Speaking Spanish).
By the end of my pay period,"" Delgado says, ""I'm skipping meals so that my kids can eat."" Poor wages aren't the only issue.
Lexmark workers claim they endure sexual harassment by their superiors and are given inadequate safety gear.
They're not alone.
Some 300 workers from at least three other Juarez factories are protesting similar conditions and have petitioned the state to authorize their effort to unionize.
It's not just a fight between our workers and some maquiladoras.
It's a matter of the life that everybody deserve to live.
Elizabeth Flores is a local labor attorney.
She says worker discontent has been building for some time.
After years of economic recession and a period of heavy drug violence, Juarez is finally recovering.
Today, there are more factory jobs than people available to fill them.
As industry grows in Mexico's interior, fewer people are migrating to northern states in search of work.
All this has empowered Juarez workers to speak out.
And if you talk to labor leaders and people in maquilas, they're very worries as to where the future labor force is going to come from.

And I think that sometimes a word can capture what is already in the zeitgeist, what's already out there, and can, in a sense, can mobilize people behind what's already was second nature to them and can encourage even greater acts of hypermiling in this case.

Frolleague is another one of these terms, which is a friend who is a colleague on a social networking site, such as MySpace or Facebook or LinkedIn.
And this is, as in the case of cow pooling, somewhat of an artificial term.

In the case of cow pooling, one that was made up in an act of cleverness - a very writerly sort of term, whereas frolleague seems to have come from LinkedIn, which did a study finding that 40 percent - 47 percent of users in the U.K.
mix friends and business contacts on their social networking sites, causing all sorts of problems where business contacts see stag photos and things of the sort.

And the term gives a sort of authority to pop sociology in this case, or it might be for pop psychology or many other sorts of popular pursuits that have an academic tinge but not a whole lot of academic credentialing behind them

I have a 20-year-old son who is an avid gamer.
And he wears a t-shirt and it says latency kills.
And I've always wondered, you know, like what does that mean?

And so, I asked him the other day and he said that when you're playing a game, like with your peers are all on a network on the Net all across the world, and you do an action on your computer, the time between when you do that action and when it actually takes place in the game is called latency.
And if you, you know - you can get killed during that little pause.
Wow.
It's a good term because it takes a word that is already in the language and expands upon it for something that really happens.
Well, I guess in the world of games whether it's real or it isn't is debatable, but it's a word that is called for by a community and that makes use of the language that we have in a novel way.
So, I think that that is a word that has much greater chance of survival in the long term than something like frolleague or cow pooling.
